attributes.stem:
A 52-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a two-week history of a very painful ulcer on her left ankle that has been rapidly enlarging. She reports that it started as a small pustule which formed after knocking her ankle, which quickly broke down and expanded. She has a medical history of rheumatoid arthritis, currently managed with methotrexate. She does not take any other regular medications. She does not smoke and does not drink alcohol.

On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 72/min, blood pressure 120/80 mmHg, respiratory rate 14/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. An ulcer is seen over the medial malleolus of her left ankle (Image). Her pedal pulse is palpable. There are no other lesions or lymphadenopathy, and there is no active joint inflammation.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The clinical presentation in this case is highly suggestive of pyoderma gangrenosum. This neutrophilic dermatosis typically starts as a nodule, papule or pustule which forms after minor trauma, rapidly progressing into a painful, expanding ulcer. Ulcer characteristics include undermined violaceous borders with a necrotic or exudative base. Pyoderma gangrenosum is often associated with autoimmune conditions, most commonly inflammatory bowel disease, but also rheumatoid arthritis.

The diagnosis of pyoderma gangrenosum is based on criteria including its characteristic appearance, the presence of an associated condition, and a neutrophilic infiltrate found on biopsy of the ulcer edge. Management typically involves topical or systemic immunosuppression, along with appropriate wound care.

A venous ulcer would typically be less painful, have a more gradual onset, and be associated with other signs of venous insufficiency such as varicose veins, hemosiderin deposition, or lipodermatosclerosis. Venous ulcers usually have irregular borders, a red base with yellow fibrinous tissue, and may be surrounded by hyperpigmented skin. The rapid progression and violaceous borders are not typical of venous ulcers.

Cutaneous vasculitis can present with ulceration, but it typically begins with palpable purpura or other skin lesions before ulceration occurs. Vasculitic ulcers are often multiple, small, and punched-out in appearance, with a predilection for the lower extremities. They may have a necrotic base but lack the characteristic borders of pyoderma gangrenosum.

An arterial ulcer typically occurs in patients with peripheral arterial disease and is characterized by severe pain, especially at night or when the leg is elevated. These ulcers are usually well-demarcated with a pale base and minimal granulation tissue. They often occur on the toes, heels, or bony prominences of the foot. The location and appearance of the ulcer in this case make an arterial ulcer less likely.

Squamous cell carcinoma can present as an ulcerating lesion, but it typically has a much slower progression over months to years. These ulcers often have raised, rolled edges with a crusted or scaling surface. They may bleed easily and have an indurated base. The rapid onset and characteristic appearance of the ulcer in this case are not consistent with squamous cell carcinoma.
learningPoints: Pyoderma gangrenosum is a neutrophilic dermatosis that presents as painful, rapidly progressing ulcers with undermined violaceous borders.
Pyoderma gangrenosum is often associated with systemic diseases such as inflammatory bowel disease, rheumatoid arthritis, and haematological disorders.
Management of pyoderma gangrenosum involves systemic immunosuppression and wound care, avoiding surgical debridement which can worsen the condition.
references: Maverakis, Emanual, et al. "Pyoderma gangrenosum." Nature Reviews Disease Primers 6.1 (2020): 81.
